title: How Microdosing Affects Your Relationship with Food and Eating
body:
How Microdosing Affects Your Relationship with Food and Eating

If you’re thinking about microdosing, or already experimenting with tiny amounts of psychedelics, you might notice some shifts in your relationship with food. Maybe you’re not as interested in junk food, or meals feel more satisfying. Or, on the flip side, maybe your appetite feels all over the place. Does that land? These changes can be surprising, sometimes confusing. So let’s unpack how microdosing can affect your eating habits, cravings, and the way you experience food.

What Microdosing Actually Does to Your Body and Mind

First, a quick refresher on microdosing itself. It usually means taking about one-tenth to one-twentieth of a standard psychedelic dose - like psilocybin mushrooms or LSD - every few days. The goal isn’t to trip but to gently shift your perception, mood, and cognition in subtle ways. People report improved focus, mood stability, creativity, and sometimes physical effects like changes in appetite or digestion.

Here’s the thing, your brain and gut are tightly linked. You might have heard of the “gut-brain axis” but beyond the buzzword, it’s a powerful channel where your mood, hormones, and even cravings get dialed up or down. Psychedelics, even in microdoses, interact with serotonin receptors, many of which live in your gut. So, when you microdose, you’re not just affecting your mind - you’re influencing your gut chemistry too.

That interplay can lead to shifts in hunger signals, taste perception, and digestion speed. Some people notice they eat less, others more, or just differently. If you’re curious about the science behind this, MAPS has some solid research you can check out right here.

Microdosing and Appetite: The Push and Pull

One of the most common stories I hear from people microdosing is that their appetite changes - and not always in the same way. Take Sarah, for example. She started microdosing psilocybin mushrooms three times a week. Before, she’d binge on chips and candy in the afternoons, a habit she barely noticed. But after a few weeks, she found those cravings vanished. Instead, she craved fresh fruits and nuts, meals that felt nourishing rather than just filling.

“I’m actually hungry when I eat now, not just bored or stressed,” she said. Right?! That kind of shift isn’t rare. Microdosing can tune you into your body’s real hunger cues, so you’re less likely to eat out of habit or emotion.

But it’s not always a decrease. I’ve also talked to Mark, who found his appetite increased when microdosing LSD. He said, “I noticed I’d want to eat more meals a day, but smaller portions. And my food tasted sharper, more intense.”

Both Sarah and Mark experienced a recalibration of their eating rhythms, though in different directions. The key is that your relationship with food becomes more conscious, more responsive. How does that make you feel? Maybe more in control, or maybe a little unsure if your body’s asking for something new.

Why Food Tastes Different on Microdoses

Ever noticed how music sounds richer or colors pop more on psychedelics? Even on microdoses, your senses can sharpen. Taste buds might get a little boost, making flavors more vivid. That can mean food feels more satisfying or even more challenging if you’re used to eating things loaded with sugar or additives.

Think about it like this: if you’ve been eating fast food or processed snacks, microdosing might highlight how artificial or bland those flavors are. Your brain could start craving real, whole foods instead. It’s not magic. It’s your senses dialed up a notch.

That’s why some people switch to cooking at home more often. I know a guy, Josh, who grew mushrooms at home using a mushroom growing kit (paid link). He said microdosing made him appreciate the earthiness and texture of fresh mushrooms in a way he never did before. Cooking became a creative ritual, not a chore.

Microdosing and Emotional Eating

Look, emotional eating is real. Stress, anxiety, boredom - they all can push you to reach for comfort food. Microdosing sometimes helps because it reduces anxiety and lifts mood, which can take the edge off emotional eating cycles.

But here’s the catch: microdosing isn’t a cure-all. Sometimes, it can bring buried feelings closer to the surface. That means you might become more aware of emotional triggers related to food, which can be uncomfortable at first. It’s like shining a flashlight in a dark room - you see what was there all along but hidden.

That’s why I encourage anyone microdosing to pair it with some kind of grounding practice. Sitting quietly on a meditation zafu cushion (paid link) for 10 minutes after dosing can help process emotions without judgment.

If you find yourself overeating or undereating because of feelings, give yourself permission to notice it without shame. The shift in awareness is the first step to rewiring your habits.

Microdosing and Digestion: What to Expect

Some folks report mild digestive changes when microdosing. You might notice your stomach feels queasy or you have a bit more gas or bloating. That’s usually temporary and tends to settle down after a few doses. Your body is adjusting to new serotonin signaling in the gut. But if you have a sensitive stomach, keep an eye on it.

On the flip side, many people experience improved digestion over time. Microdosing might help regulate constipation or improve gut motility, though these effects aren’t guaranteed. If you want to support your gut further, adding an aromatherapy essential oil diffuser (paid link) like this one with calming scents such as peppermint or ginger can soothe your digestive system.

Practical Tips for Navigating Food and Eating While Microdosing

  • Keep a Food and Microdosing Journal: Write down what you eat, how it tastes, your hunger levels, and any digestive symptoms. Tracking helps you notice patterns and adjust accordingly.
  • Plan Meals Ahead: When microdosing changes your appetite, it helps to have nourishing options ready. This prevents reaching for processed snacks out of habit.
  • Stay Hydrated: Psychedelics can shift your hydration needs. Drinking enough water impacts digestion and energy levels.
  • Be Patient: Your body might take a few weeks to settle into new rhythms. Don’t panic if your appetite fluctuates.
  • Check In With Your Body: Ask yourself if you’re really hungry or if emotions are driving your cravings. It’s a moment of mindfulness, nothing more complicated.
  • Don’t Force It: If certain foods feel off or unappealing, don’t push yourself. Let your palate guide you.

The Role of Intention and Awareness

Honestly, how you approach microdosing shapes your experience with food as much as the substance itself. If you’re curious and kind toward yourself, you’ll notice changes without judgment. If you’re trying to “fix” your diet overnight, that’s a recipe for frustration.

Microdosing opens a door to awareness. What you do after stepping through that door is what counts. Are you willing to listen to your body? To try new flavors? To slow down and savor meals? Those small choices add up.

If you want a great read that explores the history and effects of psychedelics on perception, I recommend The Doors of Perception by Aldous Huxley (paid link). It’s a classic that still resonates today.

When to Seek Guidance

If your relationship with food feels deeply troubled - maybe disordered eating or unmanaged emotional distress - microdosing is not a substitute for professional help. Reach out to a therapist or nutritionist who understands these issues. You can start by exploring resources at places like Johns Hopkins Medicine for credible information.

Microdosing is a tool, not a magic wand. How you use it matters a lot.

Final Thoughts: Your Food Journey with Microdosing

Look, changing how you eat and relate to food takes time. Microdosing might speed up that process or make it feel more vivid. You might find yourself craving healthier meals, or simply enjoying food more fully. Or you might face moments of confusion and discomfort as your body adjusts. Both are okay.

Keep your expectations realistic. Be gentle with yourself. And remember, this is about learning to listen to your body better, not controlling it perfectly.

If you want to explore more about how psychedelics intersect with wellness, check out my other writings on The Microdose section of The Quiet Medicine. And if you ever want to reach out or learn more about my work, visit kalesh.love.
